blurb: A graceful small understory maple of mountain canyons — slender red-tinged twigs, crisp lobed leaves turning clear yellow in fall, and showy red samaras; happy in the dappled shade few natives tolerate.
sun: Part shade to full sun (takes more shade than most natives)
water: Moderate; prefers some moisture — not for the driest zones
seasons: Lobed leaves (red-tinged in spring) turning clear yellow in fall; showy red samaras; smooth reddish bark
wildlife: Samaras and cover for birds and small mammals; larval host

care.hardiness: A montane native of the Colorado Rockies, fully cold-hardy through Boulder's 5b–6a winters; in our region it grows from about 5,000 ft up to treeline.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out container or balled-and-burlapped plants in spring or early fall.[2] Unlike the xeric bigtooth maple, oak, and piñon, this is a streamside montane species — site it where it will get steady moisture and some shelter, not a hot dry exposure, and keep new plants watered well through establishment.[1][3]
care.sun: Tolerates partial to fairly deep shade and naturally grows under a canopy, though it is denser in more sun; on the Front Range give it afternoon shade — its leaves scorch in open, hot, dry, windy sites.[1][3][2]
care.soil: Moist, well-drained, rocky soils; a typical understory and ravine plant.[2]
care.water: The honest exception among these four — NOT a xeric tree. It favors moderately damp sites, streambanks, and canyon bottoms, so on the semi-arid Front Range it needs supplemental water and is poorly suited to a dry, unirrigated zone.[1][3]
care.spacing: A small tree or large shrub, roughly 10–30 ft tall with a 10–15 ft spread and slender spreading branches; often multi-stemmed.[2]
care.propagation: Notoriously hard from seed — the samaras need a long warm-then-cold treatment: about 180 days of warm stratification at 68–86°F followed by ~180 days of cold-moist stratification at 37–41°F.[1][2] Collect samaras once they turn yellow-to-reddish-brown and the seeds inside are firm and dark brown; provide shade for seedling establishment.[2][1]
care.harvest: Grown for its foliage, which turns yellow to reddish-orange from late August into fall (timing varies with moisture).[2][1]

edible.summary: Not a food plant — grown as a native ornamental and wildlife browse, not for eating.[2]
edible.caution: Rocky Mountain maple has no part used as food. Its sap could theoretically be tapped like other maples, but it is not a food crop here; deer, elk, and moose browse the foliage, but it is grown for ornament, not for the table. It is not considered poisonous.[1][2]
